description_en: Maria Salomea Sklodowska born in Warsaw Congress Poland (Russian Empire) youngest of five children of teachers Wladyslaw and Bronislawa Sklodowski
Oldest sibling Zofia contracts typhus from a house boarder and dies — first major loss in Maria's childhood
Mother Bronislawa dies of tuberculosis when Maria is ten — a defining trauma that influences her lifelong rejection of formal religion
Completes secondary education at the Russian lycee in Warsaw with a gold medal — top of her class despite Russian cultural suppression of Polish students
Unable to attend university in Russia Maria takes a post as governess to fund her sister Bronyas medical studies in Paris — part of a sisterly pact
Falls in love with the son of the family she works for; the relationship is forbidden by his family due to her social class ending in heartbreak
Participates clandestinely in the nationalist Flying University in Warsaw — an underground institution offering education to women in defiance of Russian rule
Leaves Warsaw for Paris at age 24 following her sister Bronislawa to study at the Sorbonne. Begins using the French name Marie
Graduates first in her class in physical sciences from the Sorbonne — remarkable for any student exceptional for a woman
Commissioned by the Society for the Encouragement of National Industry to investigate the magnetic properties of various steels — her first professional scientific engagement
Introduced to Pierre Curie by Polish physicist Jozef Wierusz-Kowalski who thought Pierre could help her find laboratory space. Their mutual passion for science draws them together
Completes a second degree in mathematics placing second among all candidates. Begins work in Gabriel Lippmanns research laboratory
Marries Pierre Curie on July 26 1895. They honeymoon by bicycle through the French countryside. The marriage begins one of historys most celebrated scientific partnerships
Henri Becquerels discovery of uranium rays becomes her doctoral thesis topic. She begins studying other elements for similar emissions — soon coining the word radioactivity
First daughter Irene born September 12. Despite new motherhood Marie barely slows her research pace
Discovers that thorium also emits radiation and that radiation intensity depends on quantity of element not molecular arrangement — her most important theoretical insight
Announces discovery of a new radioactive element named polonium after her homeland Poland — a political act as much as a scientific one
Announces discovery of radium named after the radioactivity phenomenon she herself coined
Appointed lecturer in physics at the Ecole Normale Superieure for girls in Sevres — introduces experimental demonstrations as a teaching method
After four years of back-breaking labor processing tons of pitchblende ore succeeds in isolating one decigram of pure radium chloride
Defends doctoral thesis Recherches sur les substances radioactives — becomes the first woman in France to earn a physics doctorate
Shares the 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics with Pierre Curie and Henri Becquerel for their joint research on radioactivity. First woman to win a Nobel Prize
Second daughter Eve Denise born December 6. Eve will grow up to become a journalist and biographer writing a celebrated biography of her mother
Pierre is struck by a horse-drawn wagon on a Paris street on April 19 and killed instantly. Her partner in life and science is gone
Accepts Pierres chair at the Sorbonne — becomes the first woman in France to hold a professorial position in the universitys history
Pierre Paul Emile Roux of the Pasteur Institute proposes creating a Radium Institute after the University of Paris fails to provide Curie with an adequate laboratory
Successfully isolates pure metallic radium — previously only obtained as a salt compound — the work anchoring her second Nobel Prize
A romantic relationship with physicist Paul Langevin becomes tabloid fodder. Nationalists attack her as a foreign-born home-wrecker. A vicious public campaign follows

Awarded the 1911 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the isolation of pure radium. Nobel Committee suggested she not attend due to scandal — she goes anyway. Only person to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences
Suffers severe depression and physical collapse. Recovers quietly in England under a pseudonym with friend Hertha Ayrton
Designs mobile radiography units — petites Curies — and drives them to field hospitals herself treating an estimated one million wounded soldiers
Appointed director of the scientific department of the newly opened Radium Institute in Paris. Daughter Irene joins the lab
Founds the Curie Institute in Paris — one of the worlds premier cancer research centers still operating today
Tours the United States to raise funds for radium research. President Harding presents her with 1 gram of radium worth $100000
Joins the League of Nations International Committee on Intellectual Cooperation — collaborates with Einstein Lorentz and Bergson on international scientific coordination
Writes and publishes a biography of her late husband — a tribute to their shared work and life
Returns to Poland for the foundation-laying ceremony of the Warsaw Radium Institute — a lifelong dream to bring science to her homeland
Second tour of the United States. President Hoover presents her with funds to equip the Warsaw Radium Institute with radium
The Warsaw Radium Institute opens with her sister Bronislawa as director — Maries last major institutional legacy in Poland
Visits Poland for the last time visibly ill. Returns to France shortly after
Dies July 4 1934 at the Sancellemoz sanatorium in Passy France of aplastic anaemia — almost certainly caused by decades of unprotected radiation exposure
